chapters.title: Periodic Table and Periodic Properties
chapters.subtitle: Foundation of all Chemistry
chapters.overview: This chapter traces the historical development of the periodic table and explains the modern arrangement of 118 elements. It covers classification of elements, the s/p/d/f block system, element families, periodic trends in atomic/ionic radius, ionization energy, electron affinity, and electronegativity, plus reactions of sodium and magnesium and the acid-base nature of Period 3 oxides and chlorides.

highYieldTopics.topic: Periodic trends: atomic radius, ionic radius, IE, EA, electronegativity
s, p, d, f blocks and electron configuration from position
Element families (alkali metals, halogens, noble gases, etc.)
Oxides and chlorides of Period 3 — acidic/basic/amphoteric
Anomalies in IE (B<Be, O<N) and EA (F vs Cl)
Reactions of Na and Mg with water, O₂, Cl₂

topics.title: Historical Development of the Periodic Table
topics.memoryTrick: LD NaMe Mo — Lavoisier → Döbereiner → Newlands → Lothar Meyer → Mendeleev → Moseley
keyPoints: Antoine Lavoisier (18th century): First classification into metals and non-metals
Döbereiner's Triads (1829): Grouped elements in sets of 3 — atomic mass of middle element ≈ average of other two. Example: Li(7), Na(23), K(39) → (7+39)/2 = 23 ✓
Newlands' Law of Octaves (1864): 62 known elements arranged by atomic mass — every 8th element resembled the 1st (like musical octaves)
Lothar Meyer (1864): Plotted atomic volume vs atomic weight — curves showed periodicity
Mendeleev (1869): Arranged 63 elements by atomic mass, left gaps for undiscovered elements, predicted properties of missing elements (e.g., Eka-boron = Scandium). Father of the Periodic Table.
Moseley (1913): Used X-ray emission to determine exact atomic numbers — rearranged elements by atomic number
topics.modernLaw: The physical and chemical properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers.

revisionTable.contribution: Atomic volume vs atomic weight curves
63 elements by atomic mass; left gaps; predicted missing elements
X-ray emission → atomic number basis → Modern Periodic Law

examTips: Mendeleev used ATOMIC MASS — Moseley corrected it using ATOMIC NUMBER
The number '62' (Newlands) and 'every 8th' are exact details examiners check
Mendeleev's genius = leaving gaps + predicting properties of undiscovered elements

topics.title: Modern Periodic Table: Main Features
topics.memoryTrick: PEriods are Parallel (horizontal), Groups stand Tall (vertical)
keyPoints: 118 elements arranged in ascending order of atomic number
7 horizontal rows = Periods
18 vertical columns = Groups
Old system: 8 A-groups + 10 B-groups = 18 total
Same group → same valence electrons → similar chemical properties
Same group → gradual change in physical properties (top to bottom)
Same period → gradual change in properties (left to right)
examTips: 7 periods and 18 groups — memorize these numbers exactly
Elements in same group have similar CHEMICAL properties (not identical)
Physical properties still change gradually down the group

topics.title: Metals, Non-Metals, and Metalloids
topics.memoryTrick: Metals LOSE electrons (cations) — Non-metals GAIN electrons (anions) — Metalloids do BOTH
keyPoints: Metals: tend to lose electrons → form positive ions (cations). Examples: Fe, Cu, Au, Ag
Non-metals: tend to gain electrons → form negative ions (anions). Examples: Cl, S, P
Metalloids: properties of both metals and non-metals — lie along stair-step line
Stair-step line: begins at Boron (B), ends at Polonium (Po)
Metalloids: B, Si, Ge, As, Sb, Te, Po
EXCEPTION: Hydrogen is a non-metal despite being in Group 1 (far left)
examTips: All 7 metalloids: B, Si, Ge, As, Sb, Te, Po — memorize all, missing one costs marks
Hydrogen = non-metal (common trap question)
Tellurium (Te) is a metalloid, NOT a non-metal

topics.title: s, p, d, f Blocks of the Periodic Table
topics.memoryTrick: s1-2, p13-18, d3-12, f at the bottom
keyPoints: s-Block: Groups 1–2; valence electrons in s-subshell; includes alkali metals + alkaline earth metals. Example: Na = 1s²2s²2p⁶3s¹
p-Block: Groups 13–18; valence electrons in p-subshell; includes non-metals, metalloids, noble gases. Example: Cl = 1s²2s²2p⁶3s²3p⁵
d-Block: Groups 3–12; valence electrons in d-subshell; transition metals. Exhibit variable oxidation states + colored compounds
f-Block: valence electrons in f-subshell; Lanthanides (Z=57–71) + Actinides (Z=89–103); placed at bottom of table
examTips: d-Block is Groups 3–12 (NOT 1–12 — common mistake)
f-Block = lanthanides + actinides (28 elements total)
Noble gases are p-block (Group 18)

families.name: Alkali Metals
families.elements: Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs, Fr
traits: React vigorously with water to form alkalis + H₂

families.name: Alkaline Earth Metals
families.elements: Be, Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba, Ra

families.name: Transition Metals
families.elements: Cr, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn (examples)

families.name: Chalcogens
families.elements: O, S, Se, Te, Po, Lv
traits: Name from Greek 'chalkos' (copper) — most copper ores are oxides/sulfides
O, S = non-metals; Se, Te, Po = metalloids; Lv = metal

families.name: Halogens
families.elements: F, Cl, Br, I, At, Ts
traits: Gain 1 electron to complete outer shell

families.name: Noble Gases
families.elements: He, Ne, Ar, Kr, Xe, Rn, Og
traits: Complete outermost shells — almost entirely unreactive
CAN form compounds: Xe forms XePtF₆ (why name changed from 'inert gases' to 'noble gases')

topics.title: Electronic Configuration and Periodic Table Position
keyPoints: Period number = principal quantum number (n) = number of electron shells
Group number = number of valence electrons (for main group elements)
Block = which subshell holds the valence electrons

topics.title: Atomic Radius and Ionic Radius
keyPoints: Atomic radius: half the distance between two identical bonded atoms. Unit: picometer (pm) or Angstrom (Å)
Factors: number of shells (more shells → larger), nuclear charge (greater charge → smaller), shielding effect (more inner electrons → larger)
Across period (left→right): DECREASES — nuclear charge increases, shells constant, electrons pulled closer
Down group (top→bottom): INCREASES — additional shells added, shielding increases
Example across period: Li > Be > B > C > N > O > F
Example down group: Li < Na < K < Rb < Cs
Cation (positive ion): SMALLER than parent atom — loses electrons, may lose entire shell, remaining electrons pulled more tightly. Example: Na(186 pm) → Na⁺(95 pm)
Anion (negative ion): LARGER than parent atom — gains electrons, increases repulsion, cloud expands. Example: Cl(99 pm) → Cl⁻(181 pm)
examTips: Atomic radius DECREASES across a period (common wrong answer: increases)
Anion is LARGER than neutral atom (common wrong answer: smaller)
Always specify reason: nuclear charge / shielding / shells

topics.title: Ionization Energy
topics.definition: The energy needed to remove one electron from each atom in one mole of gaseous atoms to form one mole of gaseous 1+ ions.
topics.equation: Na₍g₎ → Na⁺₍g₎ + e⁻  ΔHᵢ₁ = +494 kJ mol⁻¹
factors: Nuclear charge: greater charge → electrons held more tightly → higher IE
Atomic size: larger atom → outer electrons farther away → lower IE
Electronic arrangement: half-filled/fully-filled subshells are extra stable → higher IE
Shielding effect: more inner electrons → greater shielding → lower IE
Spin-pair repulsion: paired electrons repel each other → slightly easier to remove → lower IE

trends.acrossPeriod: Generally INCREASES left to right — nuclear charge increases, atomic size decreases
trends.downGroup: DECREASES — atomic size increases, shielding increases
trends.example: Group 1: Li > Na > K > Rb > Cs

anomalies: IE(Al) < IE(Mg): Al's 3p¹ electron is in a higher-energy subshell than Mg's fully-filled 3s² — easier to remove
IE(O) < IE(N): N has half-filled 2p³ (extra stable). O has paired 2p electron (spin-pair repulsion → easier to remove). IE(N) = 1403 kJ/mol; IE(O) = 1365 kJ/mol
examTips: Definition must include 'gaseous state' and 'one mole' — examiners check this
Include state symbols in all equations
O vs N anomaly: N half-filled 2p³ is extra stable (most common exam question)

electronAffinity.definition: The enthalpy change when 1 mole of electrons is added to 1 mole of gaseous atoms to form 1 mole of gaseous uni-negative ions under standard conditions.
electronAffinity.example: Cl₍g₎ + e⁻ → Cl⁻₍g₎  ΔH°ₑₐ₁ = –348.8 kJ mol⁻¹
electronAffinity.signConvention: First EA = negative (energy released, exothermic). Second EA = positive (energy absorbed — anion repels incoming electron).
electronAffinity.trends: Across period: generally becomes more negative (increases). Down group: decreases (less negative).
electronAffinity.anomaly: F has LOWER electron affinity than Cl despite being smaller. Reason: F has extremely compact 2p orbitals — intense electron-electron repulsion when extra electron added. Cl has larger 3p orbitals with less repulsion. EA values: Cl(–349) > F(–328) > Br(–324) > I(–295) > At(–270) kJ/mol
examTips: First EA = NEGATIVE sign (energy released) — do not write positive
Second EA of oxygen = POSITIVE (+844 kJ/mol) — repulsion from O⁻
F vs Cl anomaly = compact 2p orbitals causing repulsion (not just 'size')

electronegativity.definition: The power of an atom to attract a shared pair of electrons toward itself in a molecule. (Pauling scale, dimensionless)
electronegativity.trends: Across period: INCREASES (nuclear charge up, atomic size down). Down group: DECREASES (atomic size up, shielding up).
electronegativity.metallic: Low EN = metallic character (electropositive). High EN = non-metallic character (electronegative). Cs (EN=0.8) = most reactive metal. F (EN=4.0) = most reactive non-metal.
examTips: Must mention 'Pauling' scale in answers
F = 4.0 (maximum); Cs/Fr = 0.8 (minimum)
EN INCREASES across period (common wrong answer: decreases)

topics.title: Reactions of Na and Mg, Oxides, Chlorides, and Oxidation Numbers

withOxygen.notes: MAIN product = sodium PEROXIDE (Na₂O₂) — golden yellow flame. Na kept under kerosene oil.
Only under limited O₂ or high temperature
Intense WHITE flame; MgO used in fireworks and SOS flares

basic.description: React with water → alkali (base); ionic; formed by metals

acidic.description: React with water → acid; covalent; formed by non-metals

amphoteric.description: React with BOTH acids AND bases

topics.bondingTrend: Groups 1–3: ionic oxides/chlorides. Groups 4–7: covalent oxides/chlorides. Increasing covalent character left to right.

oxidationNumbers.explanation: Oxidation number in Period 3 oxides increases from +1 (Na) to +6 (S), matching the group number
oxidationNumbers.variableOxidation: P and S show variable oxidation states by expanding their octet using empty 3d orbitals (Period 3 can exceed octet). P: +3 (PCl₃) and +5 (PCl₅). S: +4 (SO₂) and +6 (SO₃).

shortQuestions.question: State the Modern Periodic Law.
shortQuestions.answer: The Modern Periodic Law states that the physical and chemical properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers.
shortQuestions.examTip: Writing 'atomic mass' instead of 'atomic number' is the #1 reason this question loses marks.

shortQuestions.question: State Döbereiner's Law of Triads with an example.
shortQuestions.answer: Döbereiner grouped elements into sets of three (triads) with similar properties, in which the atomic mass of the middle element was approximately the average of the other two. Example: Li(7), Na(23), K(39) → (7+39)/2 = 23 ✓
shortQuestions.examTip: Always include the Li/Na/K numerical example — bare definitions are marked incomplete.

shortQuestions.question: State Newlands' Law of Octaves.
shortQuestions.answer: When 62 known elements were arranged in increasing order of atomic masses, Newlands observed that every eighth element resembled the first element in properties, similar to musical octaves.
markingPoints: Mention 'every eighth element resembles the first'
shortQuestions.examTip: The number '62' and the word 'eighth' are the two exact details examiners check.

shortQuestions.question: What was Lothar Meyer's contribution to the periodic table?
shortQuestions.answer: Lothar Meyer plotted a graph between the atomic weight and atomic volume of elements; the resulting curves showed periodicity in physical properties.
markingPoints: Mention 'atomic weight vs atomic volume' graph
shortQuestions.examTip: Don't confuse with Mendeleev's table — Lothar Meyer's contribution is specifically about graphical curves.

shortQuestions.question: How did Moseley modify Mendeleev's Periodic Law?
shortQuestions.answer: Using X-ray emission spectra, Moseley determined exact atomic numbers and rearranged elements by atomic number instead of atomic mass, resolving Mendeleev's discrepancies. He modified the law: properties are periodic functions of atomic numbers.
markingPoints: Resolution of discrepancies + new law statement
shortQuestions.examTip: Always connect Moseley's work directly to the statement of Modern Periodic Law.

shortQuestions.question: How many periods and groups are in the modern periodic table?
shortQuestions.answer: The modern periodic table has 7 horizontal periods and 18 vertical groups, containing 118 elements.
shortQuestions.examTip: Near-guaranteed 2-mark question — memorize '7 periods, 18 groups, 118 elements.'

shortQuestions.question: Why do elements in the same group show similar chemical properties?
shortQuestions.answer: Elements in the same group have the same number of valence electrons, which primarily determines an element's chemical behaviour — therefore group members show similar chemical properties.
shortQuestions.examTip: Add: physical properties still change gradually down the group — earns bonus marks.

shortQuestions.question: Define atomic radius. State its trend across a period and down a group.
shortQuestions.answer: Atomic radius is half the distance between two identical bonded atoms (unit: pm or Å). Across a period: decreases (nuclear charge increases, shells constant, electrons pulled closer). Down a group: increases (additional shells added, shielding increases).
markingPoints: Correct trend across period with reason
Correct trend down group with reason
shortQuestions.examTip: State the REASON for each trend — just naming the trend without reason loses a mark.

shortQuestions.question: Why is the cation smaller than the parent atom?
shortQuestions.answer: When an atom loses electrons to form a cation, it may lose an entire electron shell, and the remaining electrons experience a stronger effective nuclear charge (same number of protons, fewer electrons). This pulls electrons closer → smaller size. Example: Na (186 pm) → Na⁺ (95 pm).
markingPoints: Greater effective nuclear charge on remaining electrons
shortQuestions.examTip: Include numerical values (pm) — they earn extra marks.

shortQuestions.question: Why is the anion larger than the parent atom?
shortQuestions.answer: When an atom gains electrons to form an anion, the additional electrons increase electron-electron repulsion, causing the electron cloud to expand. The nuclear charge remains the same but now holds more electrons less tightly. Example: Cl (99 pm) → Cl⁻ (181 pm).
shortQuestions.examTip: The most common wrong answer is saying anion is smaller — it is LARGER.

shortQuestions.question: Define ionization energy. Give the equation for first ionization energy of Na.
shortQuestions.answer: Ionization energy is the energy needed to remove one electron from each atom in one mole of gaseous atoms to form one mole of gaseous 1+ ions. Na₍g₎ → Na⁺₍g₎ + e⁻, ΔHᵢ₁ = +494 kJ mol⁻¹
markingPoints: Correct balanced equation with state symbols and ΔH value
shortQuestions.examTip: Missing 'gaseous state' or forgetting state symbols are the top two errors on this question.

shortQuestions.question: Why does O have lower first ionization energy than N?
shortQuestions.answer: N: 1s²2s²2pₓ¹2pᵧ¹2p_z¹ — half-filled 2p subshell → extra stable. O: 1s²2s²2pₓ²2pᵧ¹2p_z¹ — one 2p orbital has a paired electron → spin-pair repulsion makes it slightly easier to remove. Therefore IE(N)=1403 kJ/mol > IE(O)=1365 kJ/mol.
markingPoints: Write electronic configurations of N and O
shortQuestions.examTip: Most common exam anomaly question — must include configurations AND IE numerical values.

shortQuestions.question: Define electron affinity. Why is the first EA negative but second EA positive?
shortQuestions.answer: Electron affinity is the enthalpy change when 1 mole of electrons is added to 1 mole of gaseous atoms to form 1 mole of gaseous uni-negative ions. First EA is negative (energy released — exothermic) as the electron is attracted to the nucleus. Second EA is positive (energy absorbed — endothermic) because the negatively charged anion repels the incoming electron.
markingPoints: Second EA = positive (anion repels incoming electron)
shortQuestions.examTip: Writing positive sign for first EA is a guaranteed mark loss.

shortQuestions.question: Why does F have lower electron affinity than Cl, despite being smaller?
shortQuestions.answer: Although smaller size usually means higher electron affinity, F has extremely compact 2p orbitals. When an extra electron approaches, it experiences intense electron-electron repulsion in the small 2p orbitals. Cl has larger 3p orbitals with less repulsion. Therefore EA(Cl) = –349 kJ/mol > EA(F) = –328 kJ/mol.
shortQuestions.examTip: Just saying 'repulsion' without mentioning 'compact 2p orbitals' is an incomplete answer.

shortQuestions.question: Define electronegativity. State its trend across a period and down a group.
shortQuestions.answer: Electronegativity (Pauling) is the power of an atom to attract a shared pair of electrons toward itself in a molecule. Across a period: increases (nuclear charge increases, atomic size decreases). Down a group: decreases (atomic size increases, shielding increases). F has maximum EN = 4.0; Cs/Fr have minimum EN = 0.8.
markingPoints: Correct trend across period with reason
Correct trend down group with reason
shortQuestions.examTip: Must mention 'Pauling' and include numerical EN values.

shortQuestions.question: What is an amphoteric oxide? Give an example with equations.
shortQuestions.answer: An amphoteric oxide can react with both acids and bases. Example: Al₂O₃. With acid: Al₂O₃ + 6HCl → 2AlCl₃ + 3H₂O. With base: Al₂O₃ + 2NaOH → 2NaAlO₂ + H₂O.
markingPoints: Definition: reacts with both acid and base
Both equations with correct products and state symbols
shortQuestions.examTip: Product with NaOH is NaAlO₂ (NOT NaAlO₃ — very common mistake).

shortQuestions.question: What are the main products when Na reacts with excess oxygen?
shortQuestions.answer: When Na burns in excess oxygen, the MAIN product is sodium peroxide: 2Na₍s₎ + O₂₍g₎ → 2Na₂O₂₍s₎. Under limited O₂: 4Na + O₂ → 2Na₂O (sodium oxide). Na is stored under kerosene oil to prevent this reaction in air.
shortQuestions.examTip: Na₂O₂ is the MAIN product in excess O₂ — writing Na₂O is the most common error.

shortQuestions.question: Name the 7 metalloids and explain what the stair-step line represents.
shortQuestions.answer: Metalloids: B, Si, Ge, As, Sb, Te, Po. The stair-step line is a diagonal line from Boron (B) to Polonium (Po) that separates metals (left) from non-metals (right). Elements along this line have properties of both metals and non-metals.
shortQuestions.examTip: Missing even ONE metalloid costs the full mark.

shortQuestions.question: Why do P and S show variable oxidation states?
shortQuestions.answer: P and S are Period 3 p-block elements with available empty 3d orbitals. They can promote electrons into these d orbitals, expanding their valence shell beyond 8 electrons (octet expansion). P: +3 (PCl₃) and +5 (PCl₅). S: +4 (SO₂) and +6 (SO₃).
shortQuestions.examTip: This concept (3d orbital expansion) is unique to Period 3+ elements — Period 2 (like N, O) cannot do this.

shortQuestions.question: Compare the reactivity of Na and Mg and give reasons.
shortQuestions.answer: Na is more reactive than Mg because: (1) Na has only 1 valence electron (easier to lose) while Mg has 2; (2) Na has lower ionization energy (IE₁=494 kJ/mol) vs Mg (IE₁+IE₂ needed); (3) Na has higher metallic character. Na reacts vigorously with cold water; Mg reacts slowly with cold water but vigorously with steam.
shortQuestions.examTip: Always give the chemical REASON, not just state 'Na is more reactive.'

mcqs.question: Which scientist is credited with determining exact atomic numbers of elements using X-ray emission?
mcqs.explanation: Henry Moseley (1913) used X-ray emission spectra to determine exact atomic numbers and modified the Periodic Law to state that properties are periodic functions of atomic numbers, not atomic masses.

whyWrong.A: Mendeleev arranged by atomic mass, not atomic number
whyWrong.B: Newlands observed octaves in 62 elements by atomic mass
whyWrong.D: Meyer plotted atomic weight vs atomic volume curves

mcqs.question: In Döbereiner's triads, the atomic mass of the middle element of Li, Na, K is:
mcqs.explanation: Average of Li(7) and K(39) = (7+39)/2 = 46/2 = 23, which equals the atomic mass of Na. This confirms Döbereiner's law.

mcqs.question: The Modern Periodic Law states that properties of elements are periodic functions of their:
mcqs.explanation: Moseley's Modified Periodic Law: 'The physical and chemical properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers.'

whyWrong.A: This was Mendeleev's basis — now superseded
whyWrong.C: Neutrons do not determine chemical properties
whyWrong.D: Shells are a consequence of atomic number, not the basis

mcqs.question: How many periods and groups are there in the modern periodic table?
mcqs.explanation: The modern periodic table has exactly 7 horizontal rows (periods) and 18 vertical columns (groups), containing 118 elements.

mcqs.question: The term 'halogen' means:
mcqs.explanation: 'Halogen' is derived from Greek meaning 'salt-former' because these elements readily react with alkali and alkaline earth metals to form salts (e.g., NaCl, MgCl₂).

mcqs.question: Transition metals belong to which block of the periodic table?
mcqs.explanation: Transition metals occupy Groups 3–12 and their valence electrons are in the d-subshell, placing them in the d-block.

mcqs.question: Which ion is LARGER than its parent neutral atom?
mcqs.explanation: Cl⁻ gains one electron, increasing electron-electron repulsion and expanding the electron cloud. Anions are always larger than their parent atoms. Na⁺, Mg²⁺, Al³⁺ are cations — smaller than parent atoms.

mcqs.question: The electron affinity of F is less negative than Cl because:
options: A. F has lower nuclear charge than Cl
B. F has a larger atomic radius than Cl
C. F has compact 2p orbitals causing intense electron-electron repulsion
D. F has a completely filled valence shell
mcqs.explanation: Despite F being smaller (which normally increases EA), its extremely compact 2p orbitals create intense inter-electron repulsion when an additional electron is added, reducing energy released. Cl's larger 3p orbitals accommodate the electron with less repulsion.

whyWrong.A: F has lower nuclear charge but that is not the reason
whyWrong.B: F has SMALLER radius, not larger
whyWrong.D: F has 7 valence electrons, not a full shell

mcqs.question: Which oxide of Period 3 is amphoteric?
mcqs.explanation: Al₂O₃ is the classic amphoteric oxide — it reacts with HCl (acting as base) and with NaOH (acting as acid). It represents the boundary between metallic and non-metallic character in Period 3.

mcqs.question: When Al₂O₃ reacts with NaOH, the product formed is:
mcqs.explanation: Al₂O₃ + 2NaOH → 2NaAlO₂ + H₂O. Sodium aluminate (NaAlO₂) is the correct product when amphoteric Al₂O₃ acts as an acid with NaOH.

whyWrong.C: Al(OH)₃ forms from AlCl₃ with water

mcqs.question: The oxidation state of sulfur in SO₃ is:
mcqs.explanation: In SO₃: x + 3(–2) = 0 → x = +6. All six valence electrons of sulfur are used in bonding with three oxygen atoms.

whyWrong.A: +2 = oxidation state of S in SCl₂
whyWrong.B: +4 = oxidation state of S in SO₂

mcqs.question: Going down Group 1, the ionization energy:
mcqs.explanation: Going down Group 1 (Li→Na→K→Rb→Cs), atomic size increases and shielding effect increases. Both factors make it easier to remove the outermost electron, so IE decreases: Li > Na > K > Rb > Cs.

whyWrong.D: No such pattern exists for Group 1

mcqs.question: Which element in Period 3 reacts MOST vigorously with water?
mcqs.explanation: Sodium (Na) is a Group 1 alkali metal with one valence electron and the lowest ionization energy in Period 3. It reacts vigorously with cold water: 2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂.

whyWrong.A: Mg reacts slowly with cold water, vigorously only with steam
whyWrong.B: Al forms protective oxide layer and barely reacts
whyWrong.D: Si does not react with water under normal conditions

mcqs.question: The electron configuration 1s²2s²2p⁶3s²3p¹ belongs to which element?
mcqs.explanation: Counting electrons: 2+2+6+2+1 = 13 → Atomic number 13 = Aluminium (Al). Period 3 (3rd shell), Group 13 (3 valence electrons).

mcqs.question: Across a period from left to right, electronegativity:
options: A. Decreases due to increasing atomic size
B. Increases due to increasing nuclear charge and decreasing atomic size
D. Increases due to increasing atomic size
mcqs.explanation: Across a period, nuclear charge increases while atomic size decreases. Both factors cause the bonded electron pair to be attracted more strongly toward the atom, increasing electronegativity.

whyWrong.A: Atomic size DECREASES across a period
whyWrong.D: Atomic size actually decreases, not increases

mcqs.question: The electronegativity of fluorine on the Pauling scale is:
mcqs.explanation: Fluorine has the highest electronegativity of all elements on the Pauling scale = 4.0. It is the smallest atom in its group with the highest effective nuclear charge for its size.

whyWrong.B: 4.5 exceeds the maximum on Pauling scale

mcqs.question: Magnesium reacts with steam to produce:
mcqs.explanation: Mg₍s₎ + 2H₂O₍g₎ → MgO₍s₎ + 2H₂₍g₎. With steam, Mg produces magnesium oxide and hydrogen gas. Cold water gives Mg(OH)₂.

whyWrong.A: Mg(OH)₂ is produced with COLD water, not steam

mcqs.question: Which Period 3 element forms sodium peroxide when burned in excess oxygen?
mcqs.explanation: Sodium (Na) uniquely forms sodium peroxide (Na₂O₂) as the main product: 2Na + O₂ → 2Na₂O₂. Mg forms MgO; Al forms Al₂O₃; Si forms SiO₂.

mcqs.question: P and S show variable oxidation states because they can:
options: A. Lose electrons from the s subshell
B. Expand their octet using empty 3d orbitals
C. Form ionic compounds with variable charges
D. Accept electrons from the 3p subshell
mcqs.explanation: Phosphorus and Sulfur (Period 3) have available empty 3d orbitals. They can promote electrons into these d orbitals, expanding their valence shell beyond 8 electrons, allowing multiple oxidation states.

whyWrong.A: s subshell electrons are not the variable factor
whyWrong.C: P and S form predominantly covalent compounds

mcqs.question: The metallic character of elements across a period from left to right:
mcqs.explanation: As nuclear charge increases across a period, it becomes harder for atoms to lose electrons. The tendency to lose electrons (metallic character) therefore decreases from left to right.

whyWrong.D: No such pattern — it monotonically decreases

mcqs.question: Chalcogens are elements of Group:
mcqs.explanation: Chalcogens are Group 16 elements: O, S, Se, Te, Po, Lv. The name comes from Greek 'chalkos' meaning copper, as most copper ores are oxides or sulfides.

whyWrong.A: Group 15 = Pnictogens (N, P, As, Sb, Bi)

mcqs.question: Noble gases were previously called 'inert gases.' The reason this name was changed is:
options: A. They were found to be very reactive
B. Xenon forms compounds like XePtF₆
C. They were discovered to have electrons
D. They react vigorously with halogens
mcqs.explanation: The discovery that Xenon can form xenon hexafluoroplatinate (XePtF₆) proved noble gases are not completely inert. Therefore 'inert gases' was changed to 'noble gases'.

whyWrong.D: They do not react vigorously with halogens normally

mcqs.question: Which of the following elements belongs to the p-block?
mcqs.explanation: Chlorine is in Group 17 (Groups 13–18 = p-block). Its configuration ends in 3p⁵, confirming p-block membership.

mcqs.question: The ionic radius of Na⁺ compared to neutral Na atom is:
mcqs.explanation: Na has configuration [Ne]3s¹. When it loses the 3s¹ electron to form Na⁺, the entire 3rd shell is removed. Remaining electrons are pulled more strongly. Na = 186 pm; Na⁺ = 95 pm.

whyWrong.A: Cations are SMALLER than parent atoms
whyWrong.C: Size changes when electrons are removed

mcqs.question: Why does oxygen have a lower first ionization energy than nitrogen?
options: A. Oxygen has a lower atomic number
B. Oxygen has one paired 2p electron causing spin-pair repulsion
C. Nitrogen has larger atomic radius
D. Oxygen is in the same period as nitrogen
mcqs.explanation: N (2p³) has a half-filled 2p subshell (extra stable). O (2p⁴) has one paired 2p electron — spin-pair repulsion makes this electron easier to remove than any of N's unpaired electrons. IE(N)=1403; IE(O)=1365 kJ/mol.

whyWrong.A: Atomic number alone doesn't determine this anomaly
whyWrong.C: Nitrogen has a similar/slightly larger radius than O but that's not the cause
whyWrong.D: Same period doesn't explain the anomaly

longQuestions.question: Describe the historical development of the Periodic Table. Discuss the contributions of Döbereiner, Newlands, Mendeleev, and Moseley.
keyPoints: Lavoisier: metals vs non-metals classification (18th century)
Döbereiner (1829): Triads — atomic mass of middle = average of outer two. Example: Li(7), Na(23), K(39). Limitation: applied to few elements only.
Newlands (1864): Law of Octaves — 62 elements by atomic mass, every 8th element resembled 1st. Limitation: failed for heavier elements.
Lothar Meyer (1864): atomic weight vs atomic volume graph showing periodicity.
Mendeleev (1869): 63 elements by atomic mass; left gaps; predicted missing elements (Eka-boron=Scandium). Limitation: Ar/K misplacement by mass.
Moseley (1913): X-ray emission → atomic number ordering. Modern Periodic Law: properties are periodic functions of atomic numbers.
Modern table: 118 elements, 7 periods, 18 groups.
longQuestions.diagram: 📊 Diagram: Historical Timeline — Lavoisier (18th c.) → Döbereiner (1829) → Newlands (1864) + Meyer (1864) → Mendeleev (1869) → Moseley (1913)
commonMistakes: Confusing Mendeleev (atomic mass) with Moseley (atomic number)
Forgetting Lothar Meyer entirely
Not writing Modern Periodic Law word for word
Missing the Döbereiner numerical verification

longQuestions.question: Explain the arrangement of the Periodic Table into s, p, d, and f blocks. Describe the five famous families of elements with properties and examples.
keyPoints: s-Block: Groups 1–2; valence electrons in s-subshell. Example: Na = [Ne]3s¹
p-Block: Groups 13–18; valence electrons in p-subshell. Example: Cl = [Ne]3s²3p⁵
d-Block: Groups 3–12; transition metals; variable oxidation states; coloured compounds. Examples: Fe, Cu, Cr
f-Block: Lanthanides (Z=57–71) + Actinides (Z=89–103); at bottom of table

Families: Alkali metals (Gp 1, 1 VE, most reactive); Alkaline earth metals (Gp 2, 2 VE); Transition metals (variable OS); Chalcogens (Gp 16); Halogens (Gp 17, salt-formers); Noble gases (Gp 18, complete shells, rarely react)
longQuestions.diagram: 📊 Diagram: Block layout — s-Block (blue, Gp 1–2), p-Block (red, Gp 13–18), d-Block (green, Gp 3–12), f-Block (yellow, two bottom rows)
commonMistakes: d-block group numbers: writing Groups 1–12 instead of 3–12
Forgetting lanthanides and actinides as f-block
Forgetting XePtF₆ exception for noble gases

longQuestions.question: Define atomic radius and ionic radius. Explain periodic trends across a period and down a group with reasons.
keyPoints: Atomic radius: half the distance between two identical bonded atoms (pm or Å)
Factors: number of shells, nuclear charge, shielding effect
Across period: DECREASES — nuclear charge increases, shells constant → electrons pulled closer. Example: Li > Be > B > C > N > O > F
Down group: INCREASES — additional shells, more shielding. Example: Li < Na < K < Rb < Cs

Ionic radius: size of ion. Cation SMALLER (loses electrons/shell, greater effective nuclear charge). Na(186)→Na⁺(95 pm). Anion LARGER (gains electrons, more repulsion, cloud expands). Cl(99)→Cl⁻(181 pm)
Ionic radius trends: both cations and anions increase down a group; decrease across a period
longQuestions.diagram: 📊 Diagram 1: Arrows on periodic table — → atomic radius decreases, ↓ atomic radius increases. Diagram 2: Na(186pm) → Na⁺(95pm) and Cl(99pm) → Cl⁻(181pm)
commonMistakes: Saying atomic radius increases across period (WRONG — decreases)
Saying anion is smaller than neutral atom (WRONG — anion is larger)
Not explaining the REASON for each trend

longQuestions.question: Define ionization energy. Explain factors affecting it and periodic trends. Also explain why O has lower first IE than N.
keyPoints: First IE: energy to remove one electron from one mole of gaseous atoms to form one mole of gaseous 1+ ions. Na₍g₎→Na⁺₍g₎+e⁻, ΔHᵢ₁=+494 kJ/mol
Second IE (Ca): Ca⁺→Ca²⁺+e⁻, ΔHᵢ₂=+1150 kJ/mol. Third IE much larger (noble gas core).
Factors: (1) Nuclear charge ↑ → IE ↑; (2) Atomic size ↑ → IE ↓; (3) Half/fully-filled subshells → extra stability → IE ↑; (4) Shielding ↑ → IE ↓; (5) Spin-pair repulsion → IE ↓
Across period: generally INCREASES (nuclear charge ↑, size ↓). Exceptions: IE(Al)<IE(Mg) and IE(O)<IE(N)
Down group: DECREASES. Example: Li > Na > K > Rb > Cs
O vs N: N = 2p³ (half-filled, extra stable, IE=1403 kJ/mol). O = 2p⁴ (paired electron, spin-pair repulsion, IE=1365 kJ/mol). N > O despite lower Z.
longQuestions.diagram: 📊 Diagram: IE vs atomic number graph — peaks at He, Ne, Ar (noble gases); valleys at Li, Na, K (alkali metals); dips at B (vs Be) and O (vs N)
commonMistakes: Definition missing 'gaseous state' or 'one mole'
Forgetting state symbols in equations
Saying IE increases going DOWN a group (WRONG — decreases)
Explaining O vs N without writing electronic configurations

longQuestions.question: Define electron affinity. Explain factors affecting EA and periodic trends. Why does F have lower EA than Cl?
keyPoints: First EA: enthalpy change when 1 mol electrons added to 1 mol gaseous atoms → 1 mol gaseous uni-negative ions. Cl₍g₎+e⁻→Cl⁻₍g₎, ΔH°ₑₐ₁=–348.8 kJ/mol
First EA negative (exothermic). Second EA positive (anion repels). O⁻+e⁻→O²⁻, ΔH°ₑₐ₂=+844 kJ/mol
Factors: (1) Smaller atom → greater EA; (2) Higher nuclear charge → greater EA; (3) Half-filled subshell → lower EA (N, P have very low EA)
Across period: more negative (increases). Exceptions: N and P (half-filled), Noble gases (positive EA — endothermic)
Down group: decreases (less negative). Halogen EA order: Cl > F > Br > I > At
F vs Cl anomaly: F has compact 2p orbitals → intense electron-electron repulsion when extra electron added → less energy released. Cl has larger 3p → less repulsion. EA: Cl(–349) > F(–328) kJ/mol
longQuestions.diagram: 📊 Table: Halogen EA values: F(–328.0), Cl(–349.0), Br(–324.0), I(–295.0), At(–270.1) kJ/mol
commonMistakes: Writing positive sign for first EA (WRONG — negative)
Writing negative sign for second EA of oxygen (WRONG — positive)
F vs Cl: only saying 'repulsion' without 'compact 2p orbitals'

longQuestions.question: Explain the reactions of Na and Mg with water, oxygen, and chlorine. Compare their reactivity.
keyPoints: With water: Na — vigorous: 2Na+2H₂O→2NaOH+H₂ (NaOH = strongly basic). Mg cold water — slow: Mg+2H₂O→Mg(OH)₂+H₂. Mg+steam — vigorous: Mg+2H₂O→MgO+2H₂ (different product!)

With O₂: Na — golden yellow flame, main product Na₂O₂: 2Na+O₂→2Na₂O₂ (excess O₂); 4Na+O₂→2Na₂O (limited O₂). Na stored under kerosene. Mg — intense white flame: 2Mg+O₂→2MgO (used in fireworks/SOS flares)
With Cl₂: 2Na+Cl₂→2NaCl (exothermic, golden flame); Mg+Cl₂→MgCl₂
Comparison: Na more reactive — lower IE, 1 valence electron vs Mg's 2, higher metallic character
longQuestions.diagram: 📊 Comparison table: Na vs Mg with water (vigorous vs slow), oxygen (Na₂O₂ vs MgO), chlorine (NaCl vs MgCl₂)
commonMistakes: Writing Na₂O instead of Na₂O₂ for main sodium-oxygen product
Missing the steam reaction of Mg (only writing cold water reaction)
Omitting state symbols from equations
Forgetting kerosene oil storage for sodium

longQuestions.question: Classify oxides of Period 3 as basic, acidic, and amphoteric. Explain bonding trends in oxides and chlorides.
keyPoints: Basic oxides (ionic; formed by metals): Na₂O+H₂O→2NaOH; MgO+H₂O→Mg(OH)₂
Acidic oxides (covalent; formed by non-metals): SO₂+H₂O→H₂SO₃; SO₃+H₂O→H₂SO₄; P₂O₃+3H₂O→2H₃PO₃. React with NaOH: SiO₂+2NaOH→Na₂SiO₃+H₂O
Amphoteric: Al₂O₃. With acid: Al₂O₃+6HCl→2AlCl₃+3H₂O. With base: Al₂O₃+2NaOH→2NaAlO₂+H₂O
Bonding trend in oxides: Groups 1–3 (ionic); Groups 4–7 (covalent). Reason: electronegativity increases left to right → difference between O and element decreases → more covalent.
Bonding trend in chlorides: Groups 1–3 ionic (NaCl, MgCl₂); Groups 4–7 covalent (SiCl₄, PCl₅, SCl₂). Same reason.
Summary: Na(basic, ionic) → Al(amphoteric) → Si, P, S, Cl (acidic, covalent)
longQuestions.diagram: 📊 Period 3 bar: Na–Mg(Basic/Ionic) | Al(Amphoteric) | Si–P–S–Cl(Acidic/Covalent) → arrow: increasing covalent character
commonMistakes: Placing Al₂O₃ in basic or acidic (it is AMPHOTERIC)
Product of Al₂O₃+NaOH = NaAlO₂ (not NaAlO₃)
Saying all Period 3 oxides are ionic (right-side oxides are covalent)
